text: A gastric bypass is an operation where we are going to not only decrease the capacity of the stomach to allow eating smaller amounts in a comfortable environment, we're also going to bypass a section of the intestinal tract to allow for some malabsorption so that the weight loss comes quicker and easier over time.
The surgeon identifies the small bowel and evaluates its anatomy. The small bowel is transected to create a Roux limb, which is positioned to connect with the excluded portion of the stomach.

The surgeon reconnects the transected small bowel by bringing two loops together to form an anastomosis. A stapler device is used to partially create the connection, followed by hand-sewn sutures to close the remaining part. The defect between the loops is closed to prevent an internal hernia, completing the small bowel portion of the gastric bypass procedure.

The surgeon brings the small bowel limb over the colon to the stomach, preparing it for connection to the excluded portion of the stomach. Using a stapling device, a small pouch is created from the stomach, approximately fifteen to twenty cc in size, resembling the size of a golf ball or plum, which restricts food intake to one to two ounces.

The surgeon completes the exclusion of the stomach pouch and connects the small bowel to the pouch through a hand-sewn gastrojejunostomy. The small bowel and stomach are joined by creating small holes in each, approximately one to one and a half centimeters in size, and securing them with sutures to ensure controlled passage of food.
The surgeon places two separate layers of sutures to bring the stomach together and reinforce the staple line, ensuring the area is secure and minimizing the risk of leakage.

The surgeon completes the first layer of suturing on the anastomosis, tying off the sutures before placing a second reinforcing layer under direct vision using hand-sewn techniques. An orogastric tube is inserted into the gastric pouch, which is distended with dyed fluid to test for leaks. After confirming no leakage, biologic glue is applied to reinforce the area and prevent post-operative bleeding. A drainage catheter is positioned around the anastomosis site.
